water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.

generated_text.quick_read: The Chattahoochee River currently offers good but technical trout fishing conditions with ideal water temperature and recent stocking. Expect trout to be selective due to bright, high-pressure weather.
generated_text.best_window: The best window is day1 morning because cooler temperatures, low wind, and minimal cloud cover create ideal conditions for trout to feed actively using nymphs and midges. This period aligns with the angler's recommendation for a mix of careful presentations and opportunistic streamer tactics.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 58.6°F, flow is 768 cfs, gage height is 1.82 ft, and turbidity is low at 2.4 NTU, indicating clear water favorable for trout.
generated_text.weather_setup: Current weather features bright skies with 22% average cloud cover, high pressure at 30.1 inHg, steady pressure trend, light NE winds averaging 1.6 mph with gusts up to 10.4 mph, and low rain probability with no expected rainfall.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will likely hold near riffles, runs, seams, deeper pools, and undercut banks, favoring shaded areas to avoid bright light. Use small natural flies like zebra midges and pheasant tails, coupled with stocked trout attractors such as egg patterns, squirmy worms, and Duracell nymphs. Present flies with careful drifts and less aggression due to high light and pressure; focus on clean presentations near structure and cover.
